content:
Which one of the following options is the correct Fourier series of the periodic function $f(x)$ described below:

$$ f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl} 0 & \text { if }-2 < x < -1 \\ 2 k & \text { if }-1 < x < 1 \text {; period }=4 \\ 0 & \text { if }-1 < x < 2 \end{array}\right. $$
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} a_0 & =\frac{1}{L} \int_{-L}^L f(x) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int_{-2}^2 f(x) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \int_0^2 f(x) d x \\ & =\int_0^1 2 k d x=2 k \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { }\\ &\begin{aligned} f(x) & =\text { even function } \\So\,\,\,\, b_n & =0 \\ \text { Period } & =4 \text { i.e. i.n }(-2,2) \\ L & =2 \\ a_n & =\frac{1}{L} \int_{-L}^L \cos \left(\frac{n \pi x}{2}\right) f(x) d x \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int_{-2}^2 \cos \left(\frac{n \pi x}{2}\right) f(x) d x \\ & =2 \times \frac{1}{2} \int_0^1 \cos \left(\frac{n \pi x}{2}\right) 2 k \cdot d x \\ & =2 k \int_0^1 \cos \left(\frac{n \pi x}{2}\right) d x \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & =2 k\left[\frac{\sin \left(\frac{n \pi x}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac{n \pi}{2}\right)}\right]_0^1=\frac{4 k}{n \pi}\left[\sin \left(\frac{n \pi x}{2}\right)\right]_0^1 \\ a_n & =\frac{4 k}{n \pi} \sin \frac{n \pi}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { } \quad \begin{aligned}Now, \,\, f(x) & =\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \cos \left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) \\ & =\frac{2 k}{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n \cos \left(\frac{n \pi x}{L}\right) \\ =k+ & \frac{4 k}{\pi} \cos \frac{\pi x}{2}+\frac{4 k}{2 \pi} \times 0+\frac{4 k}{3 \pi}(-1) \cos \left(\frac{3 \pi x}{2}\right)+0+\frac{4 k}{5 \pi} \times \cos \frac{5 \pi x}{2}+\ldots \\ & =k+\frac{4 k}{\pi}\left[\cos \left(\frac{\pi x}{2}\right)-\frac{1}{3} \cos \frac{3 \pi x}{2}+\frac{1}{5} \cos \frac{5 \pi x}{2}-\ldots\right] \end{aligned} $$

content:
The Fourier cosine series of a function is given by :

$$f(x) = \sum\limits_{n = 0}^\infty {{f_n}\cos nx} $$

For f(x) = cos4x, the numerical value of (f4 + f5) is _________. (round off to three decimal places)
explanation:
Given the Fourier cosine series of a function:

$ f(x) = \sum\limits_{n = 0}^\infty f_n \cos(nx) $

For $ f(x) = \cos^4(x) $, we need to find the numerical value of $ (f_4 + f_5) $ rounded to three decimal places.

First, let's express $ \cos^4(x) $ in a more workable form:

$ f(x) = \cos^4(x) = (\cos^2(x))(\cos^2(x)) $

Using the double-angle identity for cosine:

$ \cos^2(x) = \frac{1 + \cos(2x)}{2} $

Substitute this into our expression:

$ f(x) = \left( \frac{1 + \cos(2x)}{2} \right)\left( \frac{1 + \cos(2x)}{2} \right) $

$ f(x) = \frac{1}{4} \left[1 + \cos(2x) + \cos(2x) + \cos^2(2x)\right] $

$ f(x) = \frac{1}{4} \left[1 + 2\cos(2x) + \cos^2(2x)\right] $

Next, replace $ \cos^2(2x) $ using the double-angle identity again:

$ \cos^2(2x) = \frac{1 + \cos(4x)}{2} $

This gives us:

$ f(x) = \frac{1}{4} \left[1 + 2\cos(2x) + \frac{1 + \cos(4x)}{2}\right] $

Simplify this expression:

$ f(x) = \frac{1}{8} \left[2 + 4 \cos(2x) + 1 + \cos(4x)\right] $

$ f(x) = \frac{1}{8} \left[3 + 4 \cos(2x) + \cos(4x)\right] $

Distribute the $\frac{1}{8}$:

$ f(x) = \frac{3}{8} + \frac{1}{2} \cos(2x) + \frac{1}{8} \cos(4x) $

So the expansion includes terms for the 4th and 5th harmonics:

$ f(x) = \ldots + f_4 \cos(4x) + f_5 \cos(5x) + \ldots $

From this, it's clear that:

$ f_4 = \frac{1}{8} $

$ f_5 = 0 $

Adding these values together:

$ f_4 + f_5 = \frac{1}{8} + 0 $

$ f_4 + f_5 = 0.125 $

Thus, $ f_4 + f_5 = 0.125 $.
